q: Plant disease is generally defined as the _______ growth or dysfunction of a plant.
Plant diseases occur due to a specific _______ in the normal life process of the plant organism.
Biotic diseases are explicitly triggered by which of the following?
Abiotic plant diseases are primarily caused by non-living _______ conditions.
Any abnormal structural or functional condition that alters a plant's appearance or creates _______ is termed a disease.
Plant disease is a physical process that negatively alters some or all plant _______.
Besides physical damage, plant diseases can significantly _______ the yield and quality of the harvested product.
Approximately what percentage of all plant diseases are caused by fungal or fungal-like organisms?
Nematodes are biologically classified as very tiny parasitic _______.
Non-infectious plant diseases are unique because they are never caused by a _______ organism.
Infectious plant diseases are heavily contrasted against abiotic issues, which stem from _______ growing conditions.
The scientific study of plant health, abnormalities, and diseases is called plant _______.
A plant disease can manifest from an interaction with a living pathogen or a non-living _______ factor.
Biotic diseases can move across a single plant and spread to adjacent plants belonging to the exact same _______.
Infectious plant problems are directly distinguished from abiotic issues, which are caused by _______ factors.
Unlike abiotic issues, biotic diseases often display distinct _______ evidence of the pathogen on the host surface.
Abiotic plant disorders include non-living conditions such as drought stress and nutrient _______.
Non-infectious plant disorders are explicitly caused by external factors rather than _______ agents.
A critical defining hallmark of abiotic diseases is that they cannot _______ from one plant to another.
Which of the following is considered an abiotic condition rather than a biotic disease?
The actual visible, physical evidence of a pathogen growing on a diseased host plant is known as a _______.
Signs are incredibly helpful diagnostic features because they provide absolute identification of the plant _______.
The visible internal or external structural changes indicating the plant's overall reaction to a disease are called _______.
Symptoms represent a direct, dynamic physiological reaction of the host plant to the attacking _______.
In diagnostic pathology, signs are distinguished from symptoms because signs represent direct evidence of the _______.
Which term describes the visible alterations or host-side expressions indicating a dynamic disease state?
Common visible symptoms of plant disease include blight, canker, decline, gummosis, leaf scorch, and _______.
Viruses can disrupt normal plant morphology, causing distinct mottling, spots, crinkling, and other leaf _______.
Microscopic agents that disrupt host structures and cause plant disease are known as plant _______.
Infectious plant diseases are exclusively initiated by living _______.
The explicit term given to any parasitic organism that infects a host and induces a disease state is a _______.
Active biological pathogens can cross plant barriers and successfully _______ all types of susceptible plant tissues.
Fungal and fungal-like organisms (FLOs) collectively trigger far more plant _______ than any other pest group.
Fungal-like organisms (FLOs) responsible for destructive downy mildews include Phytophthora and _______.
A key characteristic of highly adaptable fungi is that many distinct species can simultaneously _______ a single host plant.
The fungal genus Aspergillus is widely known in agriculture for inducing the rotting of _______.
The fungal genus Fusarium is widely documented for causing soft rots and wilts in various _______.
The aggressive fungal genus Puccinia is heavily notorious for causing destructive _______ in cereal crops.
The widespread fungal genus Rhizopus is responsible for causing blights and soft rots across _______ plants.
It is an inaccurate generalization to claim that all species of _______ are harmful to plants and soil health.
Globally, there are approximately how many distinct types of bacteria known to actively cause diseases in plants?
Plant-pathogenic bacteria exhibit peak biological activity and rapid propagation in wet and humid _______.
Bacterial cells can be splashed across short distances by _______ or carried further by wind, birds, and insects.
Members of the bacterial genus Erwinia are well-known agents that trigger _______ gall disease in diverse plants.
The species Streptomyces is known for inducing common scab in potato crops and pox disease in sweet _______.
Viruses are structurally minimal, sub-microscopic _______ pathogenic particles that require a living host cell to replicate.
Unlike larger spores of fungi or flagellated bacteria, plant viruses cannot swim or blow freely and rely heavily on environmental vectors or _______.
Among all environmental factors and organisms, the most widespread biological vectors of plant viruses are _______.
Viral symptoms known as ringspots are characterized by chlorotic circular patterns intermingled with normal plant _______.
Diseases categorized as mosaics show a clear intermingling of light green and dark green patches on the _______.
Nematodes are simple, unsegmented, multi-cellular animals properly classified as _______.
The vast majority of nematode species that target and cause damage to agricultural plants are _______.
Plant-parasitic nematodes possess a specialized, needle-like piercing mouthpart called a _______.
Although crucial for feeding, discovering a stylet under a microscope is a key structural _______ sign for plant parasitic species.
Devastating plant diseases caused by infectious pathogens carry a long history of deeply destabilizing human society and _______ development.
The three modern, foundational strategies to suppress or eradicate plant diseases are biological, physiological, and _______ methods.
Biological control framework operates explicitly by introducing or supporting beneficial living _______.
A principal mechanism of biocontrol involves selecting an antagonist agent that can actively _______ the target pathogen.
Physical control methods focus heavily on exploiting or exceeding the natural physical _______ limits of the disease-causing agents.
Synthetic chemicals deployed to eradicate target pests can be applied straight onto seeds, foliage, flowers, soil, or harvested _______.
Pathology divides all animal diseases into two broad diagnostic groups: infectious diseases and _______ diseases.
By absolute medical definition, non-infectious diseases can never _______ from one person to another.
Non-infectious diseases are triggered by non-living intrinsic or extrinsic factors, including aging, genetics, lifestyle, and _______.
The chronic pathology of most human cardiovascular diseases (CVD) fundamentally initiates with _______ formation.
When high blood pressure damages the sensitive inner arterial endothelium, it immediately triggers a localized _______ response.
As an inflammatory lesion matures over time, lipids, white blood cells, and connective tissue calcify and harden into an _______.
An growing fibrous plaque narrows the arterial lumen, restricts regular blood flow downstream, and causes blood pressure to _______.
The progressive clinical hardening and loss of elasticity in arteries due to chronic atheroma build-up is called _______.
If coronary arteries become heavily occluded, they fail to deliver sufficient _______ blood, forcing myocardial tissues into anaerobic stress.
Vital oxygen and key metabolic nutrients are continuously distributed to the active myocardium via the specialized _______ veins.
Classic clinical warning signs of a myocardial infarction include acute shortness of breath, heavy sweating, and severe chest pain known as _______.
A cerebrovascular accident or stroke is explicitly caused by a sudden disruption of normal blood flow to an area of the _______.
A heart attack occurs due to an interruption of the normal blood supply to an area of the _______.
A sudden, massive blood blockage occurring inside one of the primary arteries leading directly to the brain causes a severe _______.
If a microscopic blood clot restricts blood flow inside smaller peripheral cerebral arterioles, the localized tissue starvation can lead to _______.
The common acute diagnostic symptoms of a stroke include dizziness, confusion, slurred speech, numbness, and blurred _______.
In severe, highly destructive strokes, patients often display hemiplegia, which is a profound _______ on one side of the body.
Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is an dangerous blood clot condition that most frequently manifests in the deep veins of the _______.
Epidemiological factors for cardiovascular diseases are divided into non-modifiable traits and factors that are completely _______.
The three major non-modifiable risk factors for developing cardiovascular diseases are age, family genetics, and _______.
As an individual ages, human blood vessels naturally undergo physical changes where they lose elasticity and widen _______.
Statistically, due to hormonal and biological profiles, pre-menopausal men face a higher baseline risk of coronary heart disease than _______.
The long-term physiological development of atheromas and _______ is directly linked to almost all types of CVD.
Unlike genetic traits, an individual's chosen dietary habits, routines, and overall _______ can profoundly alter their long-term atherosclerosis risk.
Large-scale epidemiological studies definitively tie advanced CVD risk to sedentary habits, poor diet, smoking, and elevated blood _______.
Statistical analysis confirms that chronic smokers are vastly more likely to develop advanced atherosclerosis than non-smokers with a similar _______.
Chemical compounds present in inhaled tobacco smoke directly cause systemic arteries to _______ and blood pressure to rise.
Inhaling tobacco smoke damages vascular linings, accelerating lipid deposition and elevating the overall risk of _______.
Beyond structural vascular damage, smoking fundamentally alters the plasma balance of lipid-carrying _______.
A highly inactive, sedentary lifestyle severely compounds health risks and removes factors that protect against _______ disease.
Remaining entirely sedentary is a modifiable risk factor known to induce clinical obesity, high blood pressure, and type II _______.
Engaging in structured, regular physical exercise acts as a powerful preventative tool against these adverse health _______.
Consistent aerobic exercise works biochemically to decrease total plasma _______, optimize lipoprotein ratios, and alleviate mental stress.
An ideal, healthy adult blood pressure reading during cardiac relaxation (diastole) is roughly around _______.
An ideal, healthy adult blood pressure reading during active ventricular contraction (systole) is roughly around _______.
If a patient's resting blood pressure consistently evaluates above 140/90 mmHg, they are formally diagnosed with _______.
Regularly measuring arterial blood pressure gives a rapid clinical indication of the functional state of the heart and _______.
Carrying excess adipose tissue and being clinically overweight directly compounds the metabolic risk of developing chronic _______.
Chronically elevated blood pressure accelerates vascular disease by increasing the risk of mechanical damage to the blood vessel _______.
